section.title: Ministry Begins in Galilee
section.sectionRationale: With John imprisoned, Jesus settles in Capernaum and launches His public ministry in Galilee, the region Isaiah foretold would see a great light. He takes up John's message of the kingdom, calls His first disciples from their nets, and moves through Galilee teaching, preaching, and healing, drawing enormous crowds. The section shows the dawn of the kingdom in word and deed.
section.thesis: The light Isaiah promised dawns as Jesus preaches the nearness of the kingdom in darkened Galilee, summons ordinary fishermen to leave everything and follow Him, and demonstrates the kingdom's arrival by healing every kind of sickness, so that the reign of God is announced and displayed together.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Jesus withdraws to Galilee, fulfilling Isaiah's light in the land of Zebulun and Naphtali; He begins to preach the kingdom; He calls the first four disciples; and He teaches, preaches, and heals throughout the region as crowds gather.

units.label: A light in Galilee
units.note: the land of the shadow of death

verses.text: When Jesus heard that John had been imprisoned, He withdrew to Galilee.
Leaving Nazareth, He went and lived in Capernaum, which is by the sea in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali,
to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah:
“Land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali, the Way of the Sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles,
the people living in darkness have seen a great {{phos|light}}; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, a light has dawned.”
From that time on Jesus began to preach, “{{metanoeo|Repent}}, for the kingdom of heaven is near.”
As Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee, He saw two brothers, Simon called Peter and his brother Andrew. They were casting a net into the sea, for they were fishermen.
“Come, {{akoloutheo|follow}} Me,” Jesus said, “and I will make you fishers of men.”
And at once they left their nets and followed Him.
Going on from there, He saw two other brothers, James son of Zebedee and his brother John. They were in a boat with their father Zebedee, mending their nets. Jesus called them,
and immediately they left the boat and their father and followed Him.
Jesus went throughout Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the {{euangelion|gospel}} of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people.

News about Him spread all over Syria, and people brought to Him all who were ill with various diseases, those suffering acute pain, the demon-possessed, those having seizures, and the paralyzed, and He healed them.
Large crowds followed Him, having come from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan.

phos.gloss: Light. Isaiah promised that the darkened, half-Gentile north would see a great light; Matthew declares that light dawns in the ministry of Jesus, who elsewhere calls Himself the light of the world, John 8:12.

metanoeo.gloss: To change one's mind, turn around, repent. Jesus opens His ministry with the same summons as John, a decisive turning toward God demanded by the arrival of His reign.

akoloutheo.gloss: To follow, come after, accompany as a disciple. More than walking behind, it means attaching one's whole life to Jesus as Master; the fishermen leave nets and family to follow, the pattern of all discipleship.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, glad tidings, the announcement of a King's saving reign. The gospel of the kingdom is the good news that God's promised rule has arrived in Jesus, proclaimed and displayed in His teaching, preaching, and healing.

basic.context: When John is arrested, Jesus moves north and makes Capernaum, a lakeside town in Galilee, His base. Matthew sees this as fulfilling Isaiah's promise that a great light would dawn on that darkened, half-Gentile region. Jesus begins to preach the same message as John, calls two pairs of fishermen brothers to follow Him, and travels Galilee teaching, announcing the kingdom, and healing every kind of sickness, until crowds pour in from every direction.
basic.hermeneutics: This section is a summary of the launch of Jesus' ministry, showing its shape: word and deed together. Notice that the kingdom is both proclaimed, in preaching and teaching, and demonstrated, in healing. Notice too the immediate, whole-life response of the first disciples, which Matthew holds up as the model of what following Jesus means.
basic.originalLanguages: The light Isaiah foretold has come. Jesus calls people to repent, to turn around, because the kingdom has arrived. He calls the fishermen to follow, to attach their whole lives to Him as disciples, and they do. And He proclaims the gospel of the kingdom, the good news that God's saving reign has drawn near in Him.
basic.typology: The light dawning in Galilee of the Gentiles hints that this King will be a light not only to Israel but to the nations. The calling of fishermen to become fishers of men echoes God gathering a people, and the healings signal the arrival of the age when the blind see and the lame walk, the marks of the promised kingdom.
basic.christ: Jesus is the great light shining in the darkness, the King whose reign is dawning. He calls disciples with sovereign authority, and they leave everything to follow. His teaching and healing reveal Him as the one who both announces and brings the kingdom of God, the fulfillment of Israel's hope.
basic.god: God keeps His promise to shine light on a people in darkness. His reign draws near not in a palace but in a Galilean town, among ordinary people and their ordinary troubles. He heals the sick and gathers the outcast, showing that His kingdom means the undoing of the curse and the mending of a broken world.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 9:1-2 is the prophecy of light in Galilee that Matthew quotes. Mark 1 and Luke 5 give parallel accounts of the calling of the fishermen. Isaiah 35 pictures the healings that mark the coming of God. And Matthew 9:35 repeats this summary of teaching, preaching, and healing.
basic.oneStory: The King has come, and His reign begins to break into the world. In a despised northern region, the light long promised finally dawns; ordinary people are called into His service; and sickness and darkness begin to flee at His approach. The kingdom of heaven is not merely announced but is visibly arriving, and its first ambassadors are fishermen who left their nets to follow the light of the world.

context.literary: This passage bridges the temptation and the Sermon on the Mount. It relocates Jesus to Galilee, restates the kingdom message, gathers the first disciples who will hear the Sermon, and describes the healing ministry, setting the stage for the great teaching block that follows in chapters 5 to 7. The summary of verse 23 will be echoed in 9:35, framing the section of teaching and miracles.
context.historical: Galilee was fertile, populous, and mixed with Gentile influence, looked down on by Judeans. Capernaum was a fishing and trade town on the Sea of Galilee, a natural hub. Fishing was hard, family-based labor; leaving nets, boat, and father meant real economic and social cost. The Decapolis was a league of Gentile-influenced cities east of the lake, showing the wide reach of Jesus' fame.
context.canonical: Matthew grounds Jesus' Galilean base in Isaiah 9:1-2, the promise of light and, in its wider context, of a child born to reign on David's throne. The healings fulfill the prophetic signs of the kingdom, Isaiah 35:5-6, and the gospel of the kingdom becomes a defining phrase for Jesus' message, later to be preached in all the world, Matthew 24:14.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the launch of Jesus' ministry as the dawning of Isaiah's promised light, to define His message as the nearness of the kingdom, to model wholehearted discipleship in the first followers, and to show the kingdom demonstrated in His teaching and healing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the beginning of Jesus' ministry and the historic call of the first disciples. Their immediate, costly following is held up as an example of true discipleship, while the healings describe the unique signs of the kingdom's arrival rather than guaranteeing the same for every believer.

debates.question: What does it mean to become fishers of men?

views.label: A call to gather people into the kingdom
views.case: Jesus turns the disciples' trade into their mission: as they once drew fish from the sea, they will now draw men and women into God's saving reign through the gospel.

views.label: An echo of prophetic gathering and judgment
views.case: The Old Testament uses fishing imagery for gathering the nations and for judgment, Jeremiah 16:16 and Habakkuk 1:15; the phrase may carry the weight of God assembling a people at the end of the age.

debates.bearing: Either way the disciples are commissioned to bring people to Jesus; their whole calling is now defined by His mission, not their old trade.

debates.question: How could the fishermen leave everything at once to follow Jesus?

views.label: A prior acquaintance now brought to decision
views.case: John's Gospel records earlier contact between Jesus and these men, so Matthew's scene captures the decisive moment of a call they were already prepared to answer.

views.label: The sovereign, compelling authority of Jesus' call
views.case: Matthew stresses the sheer power of the summons: Jesus speaks, and at once they leave nets and father, showing that His word creates the obedience it commands.

debates.bearing: Both honor the reality that following Jesus is a response to His authoritative call and involves leaving other loyalties behind to attach oneself wholly to Him.

study.originalLanguages: Matthew announces that the phos, the light Isaiah promised, has dawned on a people in the shadow of death. Jesus takes up John's cry, metanoeo, repent, for the reign of heaven has drawn near. He summons the fishermen to akoloutheo, to follow, to fasten their whole lives to Him as disciples, and at once they leave nets and family. And He goes about proclaiming the euangelion of the kingdom, the good news that God's saving rule has come in Him, a message He backs with the teaching and healing that show the kingdom is truly here.

typology.type: Light dawning in Galilee of the Gentiles
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's promise of light to a darkened, half-Gentile land, fulfilled in Jesus and pointing to the light that will reach all nations, Isaiah 9:1-2.
The gathering of a people into God's kingdom, as the disciples' trade becomes their mission in the gospel.
The prophetic signs of the coming kingdom, when the sick are made whole and the curse begins to lift, Isaiah 35:5-6.

study.christ: Jesus is the great light shining in a land of shadow, the King whose reign is breaking in. His word carries sovereign authority: He calls, and hardened fishermen abandon their livelihood and their father to follow Him. He is the herald and the bringer of the kingdom, proclaiming its good news and proving its arrival by healing every kind of affliction. In Him the darkness of Galilee, and of the world, meets a light it cannot overcome, and the long-promised reign of God takes visible form in a Galilean ministry of teaching, preaching, and mercy.
study.god: God is faithful to His promise to bring light to those in darkness, and He does it in an unexpected place, the despised north, among ordinary people. His kingdom comes not first to the powerful but to fishermen and the sick, the demon-oppressed and the suffering, showing a God who draws near to the lowly and the broken. In the healings we glimpse His purpose: not merely to forgive but to restore, to roll back sickness and death and mend His marred creation under the reign of His Son.

crossReferences.note: The great light dawning on Zebulun and Naphtali, Galilee of the Gentiles, fulfilled in Jesus.
The parallel account of the calling of Peter, Andrew, James, and John.
The prophetic signs of the kingdom, the sick healed and the broken restored, seen in Jesus' ministry.
The same summary of teaching, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing, framing this section of Matthew.

study.oneStory: With the King's ministry underway, the reign of God begins to dawn on the world. The light Isaiah foretold rises over a dark and lowly region; ordinary fishermen are swept into the mission of gathering a people; and sickness, pain, and demonic power give way before Jesus. Everything moves toward the great teaching that follows and, beyond it, to the cross and resurrection by which the kingdom will be secured and its light carried at last to all the nations.

quiz.q: Why does Matthew say Jesus settled in Galilee, in Zebulun and Naphtali?

options.text: To fulfill Isaiah's prophecy that a great light would dawn on that darkened region.
options.note: Verses 14-16 tie the move to Isaiah 9:1-2 and the promised light.

options.text: Because Galilee was safer than Judea after John's arrest.
options.note: He did withdraw after John's arrest, but Matthew's stated reason is the fulfillment of Isaiah.

options.text: Because Capernaum was His and His family's hometown.
options.note: He left Nazareth for Capernaum; the emphasis is on fulfilling prophecy, not returning home.

options.text: Because the temple leaders had invited Him to teach there.
options.note: No such invitation is mentioned; the move fulfills Isaiah's promise of light.

quiz.q: What message did Jesus begin to preach?

options.text: Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.
options.note: Verse 17 gives His opening message, the same as John's.

options.text: Follow Me and I will make you fishers of men.
options.note: That was His call to the disciples, not the summary of His preaching, which was repentance and the kingdom.

options.text: The law must be kept perfectly to enter the kingdom.
options.note: His message was repentance because the kingdom had drawn near, not perfect law-keeping.

options.text: The temple in Jerusalem would soon be destroyed.
options.note: That comes much later; here He preaches repentance and the nearness of the kingdom.

quiz.q: How did Simon, Andrew, James, and John respond to Jesus' call?

options.text: At once they left their nets, and even their father, and followed Him.
options.note: Verses 20 and 22 stress their immediate, costly response.

options.text: They agreed to follow Him after the fishing season ended.
options.note: The text stresses immediacy; they left at once, not after a delay.

options.text: They followed but kept working as fishermen on the side.
options.note: They left their nets and boat to follow; the point is a clean break.

options.text: They asked for time to consider and later declined.
options.note: The opposite; they followed immediately.

quiz.q: What three things characterized Jesus' ministry throughout Galilee?

options.text: Teaching in the synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing the sick.
options.note: Verse 23 names all three, showing the kingdom announced and displayed.

options.text: Preaching and healing, but not formal teaching.
options.note: He also taught in the synagogues; the summary names teaching, preaching, and healing together.

options.text: Debating the Pharisees and cleansing the temple.
options.note: Those come later; here the ministry is teaching, preaching, and healing.

options.text: Training the twelve in private, away from crowds.
options.note: He ministered publicly to crowds, teaching, preaching, and healing.

quiz.q: What does the healing ministry reveal about the kingdom Jesus brings?

options.text: That God's reign means the undoing of sickness and the mending of a broken world.
options.note: The healings are signs that the promised kingdom, when the sick are made whole, has arrived.

options.text: That Jesus mainly came to relieve physical suffering.
options.note: Healing displays the kingdom, but His mission centers on the gospel of the kingdom and, ultimately, the cross.

options.text: That only the sick could enter the kingdom.
options.note: The healings are signs of the kingdom's arrival, not a rule about who may enter.

options.text: That the kingdom is purely spiritual with no effect on the body.
options.note: The bodily healings show the kingdom touches the whole person and the created world.

questions: The first disciples left their nets and their father at once to follow Jesus. What might following Jesus wholeheartedly cost you, and what are you still holding back?
Jesus turned fishermen into fishers of men. How is Jesus calling you to use your ordinary work, skills, and relationships for His mission?
The light dawned in a despised, dark region, not in a place of power. Where do you need to trust that God works in the overlooked and unlikely corners of your life?
Jesus both announced the kingdom and demonstrated it in mercy to the suffering. How can your witness to Christ join words of good news with deeds of compassion?
Crowds came to Jesus from every direction with every kind of need. What need are you bringing to Him today, trusting Him to meet you?

translationNotes.note: The BSB text ends this verse with Galilee of the Gentiles followed by a dash in some printings; here the line is closed cleanly and joined to the following verse, quoting Isaiah 9:1-2.
The land of the shadow of death renders a vivid Hebrew phrase for deep darkness; Matthew applies Isaiah's promise of light to the coming of Jesus.
The Decapolis, noted in the source as the Ten Cities, was a league of Gentile-influenced cities east of the Jordan, showing the wide reach of Jesus' fame.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 4:12-25. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. The en dash ending verse 15 in the source (Galilee of the Gentiles) was removed and the clause closed with a comma. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
